name: Ansó and Roncal Valleys Loop
name_original: Valles de Ansó y Roncal – circular desde Ansó
description: This difficult 35.0-mile road cycling route from Anso offers challenging climbs and magnificent views of the Anso y Roncal valleys.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Ansó are situated in the Aragonese Pyrenees, an area defined by its dramatic natural landscapes and challenging terrain. The region features impressive defiles like the Foz de Biniés and Fago-Majones, alongside the picturesque Ansó and Hecho valleys. Cyclists encounter significant climbs, lush forests such as Selva de Oza and Gamueta, and mountains exceeding 2,300 meters. The landscape is characterized by U-shaped valleys, rivers, and waterfalls, providing a diverse environment for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Ansó The most popular road cycling route is Ansó and Roncal Valleys Loop, a 35.0 miles (56.4 km) trail that takes 3 hours 23 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers challenging climbs and views of both the Ansó and Roncal valleys. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Valle de Hecho – Refuge of Gabardito desde Ansó, a difficult 39.3 miles (63.3 km) path. This route explores the Valle de Hecho, known for its pure mountain nature and numerous excursions. Local road cyclists also love the Ochagavía – Collado de Argibiela loop from Aparcamiento de Tacheras, a 71.9 miles (115.8 km) trail leading through the Pyrenean landscape, often completed in about 6 hours 3 minutes. Road cycling around Ansó is defined by dramatic gorges, high mountain passes, and extensive forests. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels, with many routes featuring significant elevation gains. The routes in Ansó are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 3000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Ansó's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Ansó?
A: There are over 10 dedicated road cycling routes around Ansó, offering a range of experiences. The komoot community has explored these routes over 3,000 times, highlighting the area's popularity among cyclists.

Q: What is the typical terrain and difficulty level for road cycling in Ansó?
A: Road cycling in Ansó is characterized by significant climbs and rewarding views, often featuring dramatic gorges and high mountain passes. Most routes are rated as moderate to difficult, with 7 out of 12 routes being considered difficult. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e927625014/valle-de-hecho-refuge-of-gabardito-desde-anso" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valle de Hecho – Refuge of Gabardito desde Ansó&lt;/a&gt; route involves an elevation gain of over 1,850 meters.

Q: Are there any easier road cycling routes for beginners in Ansó?
A: While many routes in Ansó are challenging, there are some moderate options available. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10525199" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Peaks of Zuriza – Ansó loop from Aparcamiento de Tacheras&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 34 km route with around 550 meters of elevation gain, offering a less strenuous introduction to the region's beauty.

Q: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Ansó's routes traverse the stunning Parque Natural de los Valles Occidentales. You'll encounter impressive defiles like the Foz de Biniés and Fago-Majones, and lush forests such as the Selva de Oza and Gamueta Forest. Notable mountain passes include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/804203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collado de Argibiela&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/211384" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pierre Saint-Martin Pass&lt;/a&gt;, offering breathtaking views.

Q: What is the best time of year for road biking in Ansó?
A: The Aragonese Pyrenees, where Ansó is located, offer excellent cycling conditions during spring, summer, and early autumn. Spring brings lush greenery, while summer provides warm weather. Autumn offers cooler temperatures and vibrant foliage. However, always check local weather forecasts, especially for higher elevations, as conditions can change rapidly.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Ansó?
A: Yes, many of the routes around Ansó are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e919786918/anso-and-roncal-valleys-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ansó and Roncal Valleys Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40358821" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ansó – Collado de Argibiela loop from Ansó&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Ansó?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 50 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the dramatic natural landscapes, challenging climbs, and the sense of pure mountain nature found in valleys like Hecho and Ansó. The well-preserved medieval village of Ansó itself is also a highlight.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay near the cycling routes?
A: Ansó village, recognized as one of the 'Most Beautiful Villages in Spain,' offers amenities including accommodation and places to eat. Additionally, you can find various huts and refuges in the surrounding area, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4414444" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lizara Refuge&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2709792" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belagua Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, which can serve as rest stops or overnight stays for longer tours.

Q: Are there any specific cultural attractions or events in Ansó that cyclists might find interesting?
A: Ansó is a Site of Historical and Artistic Interest, known for its medieval essence. You can explore cobbled alleyways, traditional stone houses, and attractions like the 16th-century medieval tower and the San Pedro church. The village also hosts the 'Fiesta de la Exaltación del Traje Típico Ansotano' on the last Sunday of August, a vibrant display of local traditions.

Q: What are some of the most scenic valleys to cycle through in the Ansó region?
A: The Ansó and Hecho valleys are particularly lauded for their pristine mountain nature. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e927625014/valle-de-hecho-refuge-of-gabardito-desde-anso" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valle de Hecho – Refuge of Gabardito desde Ansó&lt;/a&gt; route offers an immersive experience through the Hecho Valley, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e919786918/anso-and-roncal-valleys-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ansó and Roncal Valleys Loop&lt;/a&gt; provides magnificent vistas of both the Ansó and Roncal valleys.

Q: Are there any routes that take me through the famous Foz de Biniés or Fago gorges?
A: Yes, the region is known for its dramatic gorges. The Foz de Biniés and Fago-Majones are impressive defiles that provide access to the village and are part of several challenging touring routes, offering an overwhelming and memorable cycling experience.
tips: Nice harbor that joins the Valle del Roncal with Ansó
Small town in the Roncal valley on the banks of the Esca river. There is a bar at the entrance next to the gas station to make a stop.

This pass, both from the Ansó slope and the Isaba slope, presents the cyclist with a long climb (13 and 12 kilometres, respectively). On both slopes, the hardest part is at the end, highlighting the final kilometer of the ascent from Ansó, with an 8% unevenness.

It is a quiet road with magnificent views of both the valleys and the nearby peaks.

Just when the road crosses the river Veral, at the height of the Zuriza refuge, there is a bathing area with a certain depth in which to cool off. The river esplanade continues parallel to the road as we climb towards the Zuriza campsite, so there are plenty of areas to get your feet wet and beat the heat on summer days.

A beautiful medieval village with traditional Pyrenean architecture. Visit the 16th-century medieval tower, the Virgen de Puyeta hermitage, the San Pedro church, and the museums. There are also several restaurants where you can enjoy typical local cuisine.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, the Valleys of Anso y Roncal circular route is classified as difficult and requires a very good level of fitness. It covers 56.4 kilometers with a significant elevation gain of 1,280 meters, including challenging climbs like the Puerto de Matamachos and the Collado de Argibiela. The final kilometer to Collado de Argibiela from Ansó features an 8% gradient.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain and scenery can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features well-paved surfaces, though some challenging segments may be steep. You'll experience magnificent views of both the valleys and surrounding peaks, traversing the scenic Roncal Valley. The landscape transitions from integrated Pyrenean views to more humanized areas, offering a diverse visual experience.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Valleys of Anso y Roncal circular route?
faqs.answer: Key highlights include the mountain passes of Puerto de Matamachos and Collado de Argibiela, offering stunning views. You'll also pass through Urzainqui Village in the Roncal valley and the starting point, Ansó. There's even a swimming area in the Veral River for a potential refreshing stop.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While not explicitly stated, challenging mountain routes like this are generally best enjoyed in late spring, summer, and early autumn (May to October) to avoid snow and ice on the high passes and ensure more stable weather conditions. Always check local weather forecasts before you go.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this cycling route from Ansó?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Ansó. As a popular village in a tourist region, there should be designated parking areas available within or on the outskirts of the village. It's advisable to arrive early, especially during peak season, to secure a spot.

faqs.question: Is the Valleys of Anso y Roncal circular route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, so while dogs are generally allowed in the region, it's not typically considered 'dog-friendly' in the same way a hiking trail might be. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured in a bike trailer or carrier, and always adhere to local leash laws and regulations, especially in villages and natural park areas.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Parque Natural de los Valles Occidentales?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Parque Natural de los Valles Occidentales. However, always respect park regulations, stay on marked routes, and be mindful of wildlife and the natural environment.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other trails. Notably, it covers significant sections of the Ruta Transpirenaica en BTT (Aisa - Roncesvalles) and the Via Sancti Martini - Chemin de Saragosse. Smaller portions also overlap with the E1. Errege bidea: Burgi - Izaba and the Senda Pirenaica - E31.

faqs.question: Are there any food or drink options along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through villages like Urzainqui Village and starts in Ansó. These settlements, especially Ansó, are likely to have cafes, restaurants, or small shops where you can refuel. The Roncal Valley is also known for its local artisan Roncal cheese, offering a unique culinary experience.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this challenging road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: Given its difficulty and length, pack essentials like plenty of water and snacks, appropriate cycling gear (helmet, padded shorts, gloves), a repair kit for punctures, a first-aid kit, and layers of clothing suitable for changing mountain weather. Don't forget your phone for navigation and emergencies, and consider a power bank.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for this 56.4-kilometer route is approximately 3 hours and 23 minutes of active cycling time. However, this can vary significantly based on your fitness level, the number and length of stops you make for photos or refreshments, and weather conditions.
